title: On Families of Filtered phi Modules and Crystalline Representations
speaker: Eugen Hellmann
abstract: We study families of filtered phi-modules associated to families of p-adic Galois representations as considered by Berger and Colmez. We show that the weakly admissible locus in a family of filtered phi-modules is open and that the groupoid of weakly admissible modules is in fact an Artin stack. Working in the category of adic spaces instead of the category of rigid analytic spaces one can show that there is an open substack of the weakly admissible locus over which the filtered phi-modules is induced from a family of crystalline representations.

proof_stages.label: Classical crystalline and weakly-admissible dictionary
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a totally ramified finite extension K/Q_p, defines crystalline G_K-representations via B_cris-invariants, defines filtered phi-modules and weak admissibility, and recalls the Colmez-Fontaine equivalence.

proof_stages.label: Kisin open-disc integral reformulation
proof_stages.summary: For Hodge-Tate weights 0 and 1, Kisin's construction converts a filtered phi-module into a vector bundle with Frobenius on the open p-adic unit disc, and weak admissibility becomes the existence of a canonical integral formal model.

proof_stages.label: Relative families and the moduli stack
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines families of crystalline representations and families of filtered phi-modules over a rigid space X, then packages the latter into an Artin quotient stack D built from GL_d and flag varieties.

proof_stages.label: Integral locus beyond pointwise weak admissibility
proof_stages.summary: Since pointwise weak admissibility does not in general produce a crystalline family, the relative Kisin construction is used to define an integral substack D_int characterized by local formal models and identified with the image of a Pappas-Rapoport period map.

proof_stages.label: Admissible locus for genuine crystalline families
proof_stages.summary: A second substack D_adm inside D_int is stated to classify exactly those filtered phi-module families that arise as D_cris of a family of crystalline G_K-representations, using relative Kedlaya-Liu-type methods adapted from phi-gamma modules to Kisin's Kummer tower.

proof_stages.label: Adic-space topology for openness
proof_stages.summary: The talk explains why rigid and Berkovich spaces do not support the needed pointwise-local constructions, introduces Huber adic spaces and their relation to the rigid G-topology, and states that weakly admissible adic points form an open subspace compatible with base change.

proof_stages.label: Proof strategy for the integral locus via relative Robba rings
proof_stages.summary: For a family over an adic space, the associated open-disc vector bundle is tensored with pointwise relative Robba rings, and a generalized Kedlaya-Liu theorem gives openness of the etale locus and local extension of integral lattices, from which the required formal model is recovered.

examples.name: Rank-one boundary family with Frobenius parameter
examples.note: For k=Q_p and X the |t|=1 boundary of the closed unit disc, the rank-one filtered phi-module with phi=t and trivial filtration is pointwise weakly admissible but the corresponding unramified characters do not patch to a continuous Galois-representation family, forcing the distinction between weakly admissible and admissible families.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes down the rank-one family with Frobenius multiplication by t, checks the Frobenius and filtration slopes are zero, and argues why the resulting pointwise crystalline characters do not patch continuously.

passes.reason: A minimal rank-one filtered phi-module with trivial filtration on the norm-one boundary is used to disprove that weakly admissible families always patch to crystalline representations.

Rank one with trivial filtration is a minimal filtered-phi family, placed on the unit-disc boundary, and is used to show pointwise weak admissibility need not come from a continuous crystalline family.

examples.name: Closed disc, open disc, and boundary non-covering
examples.note: The closed unit disc is not admissibly covered by the open unit disc together with the |t|=1 boundary; revisiting it in the adic setting exhibits the missing rank-two valuation point and makes the topological repair visible.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the closed disk/open disk/boundary situation to analyze non-admissible covering and later explains the missing adic rank-two valuation with inequalities for the coordinate.

passes.reason: The open disk plus the norm-one boundary is a boundary configuration used to show that a pointwise cover of the closed disk need not be admissible.
The open disc plus its boundary is a boundary/limiting disc configuration used to show that a pointwise cover of the closed disc need not be an admissible covering.
the boundary piece is an extremal unit-radius locus, used to show that open disc plus boundary does not give an admissible/adic covering despite covering classical points
The open disc plus the norm-one boundary is a boundary/limiting configuration used to show that covering all classical points need not give an admissible covering.
The open disc together with the norm-one boundary is a boundary decomposition of the closed disc used to show the resulting pointwise cover is not an admissible covering.
the open disc plus the norm-one boundary is a boundary case used to show pointwise coverage of the closed disc is not an admissible rigid covering
The open disk plus the norm-one boundary is a boundary-case decomposition used to show that covering all classical points need not give an admissible covering of the closed disk.
The open disk plus its norm-one boundary is a boundary case used to show that a pointwise covering of the closed disk need not be an admissible covering.
The open disc plus the norm-one boundary is a boundary configuration used to refute naive pointwise/admissible covering intuition.
It shows that the open unit disc together with the |t|=1 boundary is not an admissible cover of the closed disc, and the boundary is an extremal radius locus.
The unit disc with its boundary is a boundary-case decomposition used to show that covering all classical points need not give an admissible covering.
the open disc together with the boundary is a boundary case used to show a pointwise cover of the closed disc need not be an admissible cover

examples.name: Formal multiplicative group in the formal affine line
examples.note: The open formal subscheme G_m-hat inside the formal affine line has generic fiber the boundary of the closed unit disc, which is closed in Berkovich topology, illustrating incompatibility between formal localization and Berkovich generic fibers.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the generic-fiber construction to the formal affine line and formal multiplicative group, identifying the closed unit disk and boundary and using this to show an open formal locus becomes closed in Berkovich topology.

passes.reason: The formal multiplicative group has generic fiber the norm-one boundary and is used to show that formal openness can become Berkovich closedness under generic fiber.
The formal multiplicative group has generic fiber the unit-boundary annulus, and is used to show formal localization is not compatible with Berkovich generic-fiber localization.
its generic fiber is the unit-norm boundary annulus of the closed disc, used to show Berkovich generic fibers do not preserve the formal open/closed topology as needed
Its generic fiber is the norm-one boundary of the closed disc, used to show formal openness can become Berkovich closedness under generic fiber.
The formal multiplicative group has generic fiber the norm-one boundary of the closed disc and is used to exhibit failure of compatibility between formal localization and Berkovich generic fibers.
its generic fiber is the norm-one boundary of the closed disc and is used to show Berkovich generic fibers do not preserve the expected open-local behavior
The formal multiplicative group has generic fiber the norm-one boundary of the closed disk and is used to show incompatibility between formal localization and Berkovich generic-fiber topology.
The formal multiplicative group has generic fiber the unit-boundary locus and is used to show formal localization is not compatible with Berkovich generic-fiber localization.
Its generic fiber is the unit-norm boundary of the disc and is used to show Berkovich generic fibers can turn formal opens into closed loci.
It shows formal opens need not remain open on the Berkovich generic fiber, whose image here is the extremal |T|=1 boundary.

examples.name: Berkovich weak-admissible locus counterexample
examples.note: A simple but not fully detailed family with both Frobenius and filtration varying has a weakly admissible point set that is not locally compact, showing that the desired weak-admissible locus cannot generally be made into a Berkovich subspace.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes the existence and consequences of a Berkovich weak-admissible-locus counterexample, but explicitly says they will not go into the details of the example.
